topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Subject line: "Update on my position." My stomach instantly clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, and the carefully crafted corporate jargon dissolved into a series of utterly unbelievable claims about my friend, Sarah. Her new role, the promotion she’d been so excited about, was a complete fabrication. My hands started to tremble, the mouse slipping slightly in my grip. I reread the paragraphs, searching for any clue, any inconsistency, but it was all polished, smooth nonsense. I needed to call her, but the words wouldn't form. I just stared at the screen, my breath catching shallowly in my chest, the silence of my apartment suddenly deafening and suffocating.

[story 2]

I found out because I was reviewing Sarah’s shared project files, and the discrepancy was glaring. The official documentation for her supposed senior analyst position listed responsibilities that simply didn't align with the tasks she was actually handling. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the room tilt slightly. I quickly minimized the window, my fingers flying across the keyboard as if trying to erase the evidence. Every nerve ending felt hyper-alert, humming with an unwelcome tension. I needed to confront her, but the thought of facing her expressionless denial made my throat constrict. I paced three steps to the window, the glass reflecting a distorted image of my own wide, startled eyes.

[story 3]

It started with a misplaced memo. A tiny error in the internal distribution list, and suddenly, the entire narrative Sarah had spun about her career crumbled into dust. My heart began hammering against my sternum, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I felt a desperate need to verify, to prove this impossible situation was real. I pulled up her LinkedIn profile, scrolling through the career history, and the gaps, the unexplained transitions—they screamed at me. I leaned back in my chair, gripping the arms until my knuckles turned white. The air in the room felt thin, too scarce to draw a full breath. I couldn't seem to focus on anything but the mounting pressure building behind my eyes.
